topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I sat across the small cafe table, tracing the condensation rings on the wooden surface with a fingertip. Sarah was recounting her day with an air of practiced ease, detailing triumphs at her supposed marketing firm. But something felt… off. A tiny snag in the tapestry of her story caught my attention. When she mentioned a major client pitch, her eyes flickered down to her phone for a split second, a movement too quick, too furtive for someone genuinely excited about their career. My stomach tightened, a cold, heavy knot forming beneath my ribs. I kept nodding, offering polite affirmations, but the words felt hollow. I watched her, cataloging the subtle shifts in her posture—the slight tension in her shoulders, the way she avoided direct eye contact when the topic veered toward specifics. A deep sense of unease began to pool in my chest, pressing against my lungs. I needed to say something, but the words seemed lodged somewhere behind my teeth, refusing to form.

[story 2]

I reread the email three times. The dates didn't align. The company name I'd seen on her LinkedIn profile was completely different from the one she’d described in passing last week. My hands were slick with sweat as I gripped the printed correspondence, the edges crinkling under the pressure. Every logical thread in my mind was snapping into place, revealing a gaping inconsistency. A frantic energy started to buzz just beneath my skin, making my fingers tremble slightly. I avoided looking up from the screen, focusing instead on the pattern of the printed text, searching for the flaw in the narrative. When she finally arrived, her smile was bright, almost blinding, but I noticed the rapid blinking of her eyes. "So, how was the presentation?" I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally high. She laughed too loudly, a sharp, brittle sound that didn't reach her eyes. I just stared at the table, my jaw clenched so tightly it ached. The silence stretched, thick and suffocating, as I waited for the carefully constructed facade to crumble.

[story 3]

He leaned back in his chair, radiating an almost forced calm. "It's been a busy quarter," he said, his voice smooth as polished stone. But I saw it. The way his gaze darted toward the window every time I mentioned the quarterly reports. His hands, usually relaxed on the desk, were clasped together so tightly his knuckles were stark white against his skin. A distinct feeling of apprehension washed over me, a heavy cloak settling over my shoulders. I tried to keep my tone light, offering a casual anecdote, but the effort felt monumental. Every small detail he offered seemed deliberately vague, evasive. I started picking at a loose thread on my sleeve, a nervous habit I hadn't realized I was indulging in. My breathing became shallow, quick little catches in my throat. I felt an insistent need to pull him into the truth, to see the foundation of his story shake, but I held my tongue, the tension building into a palpable pressure in the small room.
